title: [2025] TRE 010
text:
IN THE JERSEY EMPLOYMENT AND DISCRIMINATION TRIBUNAL 

 BETWEEN 

 ROBERT
 COOPER 

 CLAIMANT 

 AND 

 (1) 
 COLLETTS CORNER LIMITED T/A THE ROYAL
 ST MARTIN 

 (2) LAUREN THOMPSON 

 RESPONDENTS 

 Reference : [ 2025]
TRE 10 

 Hearing
date : 13 
MAY 2025 

 Before: Advocate
Dr Elena Moran, Chairperson, Mrs Alison Brown and Mr Richard Renouf 

 Appearance: 

 For the Claimant: In person 

 For the Respondents: Mrs Natasha Le Dain Cyples, HR
Jersey Limited 

 JUDGMENT 

 In refusing to serve the Claimant
alcohol the Second Respondent directly discriminated against him on the grounds
of his protected characteristic of disability. The First Respondent is vicariously
liable for the action of the Second Respondent. Due to the First Respondent’s failure
to arrange training in discrimination law for the Second Respondent we order
that 100% of the compensation of £2,500 should be paid to the Claimant by the
First Respondent. 

 REASONS 

 1. 
 The First
Respondent is part of the Randalls group of companies and operates the pub in
St Martin called The Royal at St Martin (the “Pub”). Lauren Thompson was the General
Manager of the Pub. 

 2. 
 On 30 January
2025 Mr Robert Cooper filed a Claim Form in which he alleges that on Christmas
Eve 2024 he was a customer in the Pub. He says he was refused service by Miss
Thompson because of his epilepsy. He also complains that she disclosed his
epilepsy in public in front of people he did not know and told her colleagues
not to serve him. He says that the stress and upset of
the incident caused him to have a seizure the next morning. He claims
compensation and says that the seizure caused him to have a year’s driving ban.
With no disrespect to the Claimant we shall refer to
him as Alex which is the name he uses, and to differentiate
him from his father who is also Robert Cooper. 

 3. 
 In their
Response Form the Respondents say Alex was a former employee at the Pub between
March 2023 and March 2024. Miss Thompson only discovered that Alex had epilepsy
when he collapsed at work. She called an ambulance. It is said that Alex
suffered a second epileptic fit “ due to him drinking more than has been
advised by his doctor ”. The Response Form goes on to describe messages
between Miss Thompson and Alex’s parents in which the parents asked that the
staff do not serve alcohol to Alex which are said to demonstrate a caring
approach by Miss Thompson to Alex. 

 4. 
 The Response
Form continues: - 

 “ On the evening of
the alleged discriminatory act, Lauren was working at the bar when Alex wanted
to be served. She saw that he was intoxicated and refused him service. This is
her right to do so in accordance with the Jersey Licensing Law 1974, which
allows a manager the right of refusal without giving a reason. 

 Alex, in his
intoxicated state, was clearly upset at this and was screaming at Lauren quite
close to her face, which was distressing for Lauren as well. Lauren asked Alex
to step outside, again as her right was a manager, due to the disruptive nature
of his conduct and behaviour. Alex continued to be disruptive and was saying he
was going to get Nigel Godfrey (Randalls Operations Director) to ‘sort it out’,
which he did not. 

 Lauren vehemently
denies any such allegation that she told anyone in the Royal that Alex was
epileptic. She did inform him at the time that he would not be permitted to be
served alcohol again at the Royal but she would allow
him soft drinks. It is important to note that Lauren would have been completely
sober whilst Alex was intoxicated when it comes to the recollections of the
evening. 

 The Respondents deny
that there was any discriminatory action that this is a vexatious claim from a
former employee who was angry they were denied service. They counter that Alex
is an adult and has been warned by his doctor of how alcohol impacts his
condition but refuses to accept that it is an issue – this is demonstrated in
texts from his parents to Lauren and Alex’s behaviours when in the Royal as an
employee and a customer. Lauren has always been respectful and considerate in
her treatment of Alex and mindful of his condition. 

 The Respondents believe
that his epileptic fit the following day was due to his own drinking, which is
within his own control. Subsequently his driving ban is as a
result of his condition which is exacerbated by alcohol, not a
reasonable and legal refusal of service or how he was treated. The latter point
being crucial to this claim as it was Alex’s intoxication that led to him being
refused service; his disability did not contribute to the fact that he was
refused as Lauren would have refused service to anyone who appeared inebriated.”

 5. 
 The Respondents
accept that the Claimant has the protected characteristic of disability under
the Discrimination (Jersey) Law 2013 . The First Respondent also accepts
that it is vicariously liable for the actions of Miss Thompson. 

 The Law 

 6. 
 Article 22 of
the Discrimination Law says that it applies to the provision of goods and
services to the public or a section of the public. It includes refusing to make
those goods and services available and the manner in which 
they are provided. 

 7. 
 Alex’s claims
that he was refused service by Miss Thompson and that she disclosed his
epilepsy clearly fall within the scope of Article 22. 

 8. 
 The potentially
applicable forms of discrimination are direct discrimination under Article 6(1)
and harassment under Article 28. 

 9. 
 Article 6(1) says:
- 

 “A person discriminates against another person (the “subject”)
if, because of a protected characteristic, the person
treats the subject less favourably than the person treats or would treat
others.” 

 10. 
 It is not necessary for the
protected characteristic to be the sole or main reason for the less favourable
treatment if it had a significant influence on the treatment received (Flanagan
v Island Greetings Limited 147/2015 at paragraph 45). 

 11. 
 To identify if the
protected characteristic had a significant influence
the Tribunal will consider how a claimant would have been treated by reference
to a real or hypothetical comparator. The comparator does not have the
protected characteristic but is o therwise in materially the same
circumstances as the claimant. In the case of Alex there is no actual
comparator. His hypothetical comparator would be someone who presented in the same way as Alex but without the
protected characteristic of disability. 

 12. Article 28 of the
Discrimination Law sets out the test for discriminatory harassment. The
tribunal has identified a three-stage approach ( Lally v Barrista
Holdings Limited [2020] TRE 126 at paragraph 16): - 

 (a) Did the
Respondent engage in any unwanted conduct? 

 (b) If so,
did that conduct relate to the Claimant’s disability? 

 (c) If so,
did the conduct have the purpose or effect of violating the Claimant’s dignity
or creating an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating or offensive
environment for the Claimant? 

 13. 
 The burden is on
Alex to prove his discrimination claims. However, we remind ourselves that discriminatory
actions and intents are often hidden. We must consider if 
there is any unreasonable or unexplained conduct from which it would be capable
of supporting an inference of discrimination. 

 14. Article
42(1) says that the maximum compensation that can be awarded is £10,000 per
incident of discrimination including up to £5,000 for hurt and distress.
Article 42(2) says that where there is more than one respondent the tribunal
can apportion compensation in such amounts as it
considers just and equitable. 

 15. 
 In the case of Flanagan
v Island Greetings Ltd 147/2015 the Tribunal made the following
observations about awards of compensation: - 

 “117. As this
is the first case in Jersey where an award of compensation for hurt and
distress has been considered by the Tribunal, it has looked to the English
Employment Tribunal decisions for some guidance on the factors that it should take into account in reaching its decision. The Tribunal
notes that the Equality Act 2010 (the English law) refers to compensation for
‘injury to feelings’ which is broader than the compensation for ‘hurt and
distress’ allowed under the Discrimination Law. 

 118. 
 Notwithstanding that difference the Tribunal notes with approval the
case of Armitage (1), Marsden (2) and Prison Service (3) v Johnson 
[1997] IRLR 162 where the EAT established the following principles that should
be applied by Tribunals when determining the level of awards for injury to
feelings: 

 (1) Awards for injury to feelings are compensatory. They
should be just to both parties. They should compensate fully without punishing
the discriminator. The Tribunal should remain objective of their personal
feelings of indignation at a Respondent’s conduct. 

 (2) Awards should not be too low, as that would diminish
respect for the policy of anti-discrimination legislation. The Jersey
legislature has condemned discrimination and awards
must ensure that it is seen to be wrong. However awards should be restrained
since awarding sums of compensation which are generally felt to be excessive
can do almost as much harm to the policy, and the results which is seeks to
achieve, as awards that are too low. 

 (3) Awards for injury to feelings should bear some broad
similarity to the range of awards in personal injury cases. This should be done
by reference to the whole range of such awards rather than to any particular type of personal injury award. 

 (4) Tribunals should bear in mind the value in everyday
life of the sum that they have in mind. This may be done by reference to
purchasing power or earnings. 

 (5) Tribunals should remind themselves of the need to
maintain public respect for the level of awards made. 

 119. The
Tribunal has also noted the guidance provided by the Court of Appeal to
tribunals in England on the assessment of the amount of compensation in Vento
v Chief Constable of West Yorkshire Police (No 2) [2003] IRLR 102. In that
case their Lordships set down three bands of compensation in the injury to
feelings award with each band appropriate to the seriousness of the
discrimination in question: 

 (1) The top band should only apply to the most serious of
cases, such as where there has been a lengthy campaign of discriminatory
harassment because of the Applicant’s sex or race. 

 (2) The middle band should be used for serious cases which
do not merit an award in the highest band. 

 (3) Awards in the lowest band are appropriate for less
serious cases, such as where the act of discrimination is an isolated or one off occurrence. In general very low awards should be
avoided, as they risk being regarded as so low as not to be a proper
recognition of injury to feelings. 

 120. The
Tribunal has noted that the Court of Appeal in Vento , above, said that
there is ‘considerable flexibility within each of the Bands identified above,
allowing Tribunals to fix what they consider to be fair, reasonable and just
compensation in the particular circumstances of each case’. 

 121. The
Discrimination Law states that the Tribunal may not award more than £5000 for
‘hurt and distress’ when a complaint of discrimination is ‘well founded’. This
is considerably less than the amount of compensation available to the English
Tribunals in respect of ‘injury to feelings’ following an act of discrimination
but the Tribunal believes that it is still useful to set out the amounts that
it considers will form the bands of compensation for any ‘hurt and distress’
suffered in successful discrimination cases before it in the manner described
in Vento above. Accordingly the Tribunal will consider the three bands
of compensation for hurt and distress to be as follows: 

 (a) The top band, for the most serious cases, will
normally be between £4,000 and £5,000; 

 (b) The middle band, for serious cases which do not
fall in the top band, will normally be between £1,500 and £4,000; and 

 (c) The lowest
band, for less serious cases, will normally be between £500 and £1,500. The
Tribunal is aware that an applicant may consider sums of less than £500 to be
derisory compensation for the hurt and distress that they suffered following an
act of discrimination, when the value of that sum in everyday life is taken
into account, and for that reason the Tribunal will endeavour to avoid awarding
less than £500 or indeed make no award for hurt and distress at all if the
circumstances deem it appropriate to do so.” 

 The
Evidence 

 16. 
 We heard
evidence from seven witnesses namely Alex, his father
Mr Cooper, his mother Suzanne Cooper (the parents are separated) and his
girlfriend Jessica Desbois. Miss Thompson gave evidence together with two of
her work colleagues, namely Rose Newall and Jamie Parry. 

 Ms Cooper 

 17. Ms
Cooper said that on Christmas Eve Alex arrived at her house on Wellington Road
at about 3 pm. She did not know where he was beforehand. She
did not think that he had anything to drink before he arrived. Her
partner was also at the house as was a family friend who was driving. Between
them they consumed one bottle of Prosecco of which Alex had one glass. He also
drank some non-alcoholic beer. They had cheese and cold meats to eat. Alex left
her house about 9 pm. 

 18. 
 Later in the
evening Alex phoned her, he was very upset. Her phone records show that he
phoned her at 23:09 on Christmas Eve. His girlfriend brought him home and they
both came into the house. Alex was visibly upset and crying. There had been
some kind of altercation at the Pub and Alex had been refused service by the
manager because of his epilepsy. In the morning of Christmas
Day he had a seizure but no ambulance was
called. He was tired and weak after the seizure. 

 19. 
 Ms Cooper said
that Alex had his first seizure at the age of 16. She thought the triggers
could be lack of sleep, lack of food, lack of rest, dehydration and drinking if
accompanied by dehydration. She thought that his state of upset was the main
cause of the seizure on Christmas Day although in her statement she stated that
 “it could have been caused by many things ”. She said she has seen Alex have two previous seizures that were triggered
by emotional upset. She also said that Alex has not had a seizure since
Christmas Eve and that there were no seizures between July 2024 and December
2024. 

 Alex 

 20. 
 Alex said that
he lives with his father in town. On Christmas Eve he had toast for breakfast
and his girlfriend dropped him at work at La Collette. He started work at 8 am.
About half eleven he had a full English breakfast provided by his employer.
There were no work drinks. He finished work at 2 pm and
walked into town. He saw two people he knew from work at the Blue Note. He
bought one of them one drink and the other (who had been helpful to him at
work) two drinks. He did not eat or drink anything. He was at the Blue Note for
about half an hour and then he walked to his mother’s house. He got there about
3 pm. He had one glass of Prosecco with orange juice in it and three non-alcoholic
beers. He ate cheese, ham and crackers. 

 21. 
 About 9.15 he
got on the bus to St Martin’s and arrived about 15 to 20 minutes later about
9.30 pm. His father and grandparents had arrived at the Pub about 5 minutes
before him and his father was ordering drinks when he arrived. His father
bought himself and the grandparents drinks and shortly after bought Alex a pint
of lager. 

 22. 
 His father only
bought him one drink and that was the only drink he had until he went up to the
bar about 10.15 pm. His father and grandparents had left by this time. Miss
Thompson was behind the bar . Mr Parry was sitting in
front of the bar. A friend of Alex’s called RS was at the bar as was another
friend called AS. AS was there with a friend from university. Alex said that he
bought Mr Parry a Guiness and two lagers for AS and his friend. When shown the
till receipt timed at 10.19 pm Alex accepted that he had ordered a Guiness, a
Moretti and a J20. 

 23. 
 Alex said that about
three minutes later he tried to order an alcoholic drink for himself 
and Miss Thompson refused to serve him. She said that he wasn’t allowed to
consume alcohol because of his epilepsy, that she wouldn’t serve him. If he
didn’t like it he could “ fuck off ” and go
somewhere else. Alex responded that he didn’t see why he should be treated
differently as a paying customer. She told him to get out. The confrontation
lasted about a minute. He denies that his speech was slurred or that there were
any other signs of him being drunk. In response to a question from the tribunal
Alex said that he had never heard Miss Thompson swear before. 

 24. 
 Alex went
outside where he spoke to Mr Parry. Mr Parry told him it was because of his
epilepsy and there was nothing he could do about it. He rang his father. He also
rang his girlfriend. While on the phone he said he wanted justice because it
was unfair. 

 25. 
 His girlfriend
came to collect him and took him to his mother’s house where they both tried to
calm him down because he felt distraught and humiliated. On Christmas Day he
had a seizure which was caused by the stress and upset of the night before. He
was told that he had gone blue and struggled to breathe. No ambulance was
called but he reported the seizure to the hospital. He now must wait to drive
until a year after the seizure. Asked about his pattern of seizures he said
that he had several seizures around July 2024 which was due to a medication
change but he had no seizures between July 2024 and Christmas Eve. 

 26. 
 Alex has not
been back to the Pub since. This is a loss to him because its proximity to his
grandparents' house means that historically it was where they went for many
family events. 

 Mr Cooper 

 27. 
 Alex’s father
said that he arrived at the Pub with Alex’s grandparents about 5 pm. They sat
down and he bought them a drink each. He was shown a till receipt for three
drinks timed at 8.05 pm and accepted that they may have arrived much later and
maybe he left work at 5 pm. He doesn’t recall discussing Alex’s whereabout with
Mr Parry or any of the staff at the Pub. 

 28. 
 Alex joined them
about an hour later. Alex had come up from his mother’s. He bought Alex a pint
of lager and Alex joined them in the lounge area by the fire. He didn’t see
Alex with any other drinks. They were together for about an hour. He and the grandparents
left and went back to the grandparents' house which is very close to the Pub. They
left Alex at the Pub. When they left Alex seemed fine. 

 29. 
 Mr Cooper’s outgoing
call log on this phone shows multiple calls to Alex including at 5.19 pm, 7.37
pm, 7.46 pm, 7.47 pm, 7.52 pm, 7.53 pm, 8.34 pm, 10.32 pm, 10.33 pm and 10.36
pm. There was an incoming WhatsApp call from Alex at 10.40 pm that lasted 13
minutes. He remembers that he was in the kitchen with Alex’s grandfather when
he received the call. Alex was upset and frustrated at being denied service
because of his epilepsy. His tone on the phone was upset but his speech was not
slurred. 

 30. 
 In his view
seizures are caused by a combination of factors including tiredness, alcohol
and stress. He has seen Alex have seizures in the past due to stress. 

 Miss Desbois 

 31. 
 Miss Desbois
said that on Christmas Eve she finished work at 2 pm. She saw Alex at his
father’s house along The Avenue arriving at between 3
pm and 4 pm. She didn’t think that Alex had had any drinks, but she didn’t
specifically ask him. 

 32. 
 She estimates
that they stayed at the father’s house for a few hours before she dropped him at
his mother’s house. She went to dinner with her family and then to the Pub at
about 9 to 9.30 pm. She stayed for 20 minutes or half an hour and did not have
anything to eat or drink. She said Alex had two pints, one before she came and
one while she was there. In answer to a question from the panel she said that
she did not know what he had drunk before she arrived. She said Alex’s
grandparents left first, then his father and then her at around 10 pm. When she
left Alex was perfectly fine, not even tipsy and not slurring his words. 

 33. 
 When she got
home Alex called her. He was upset and crying on the phone. He said he had been
refused service because of his epilepsy which was discriminatory. She went back
and picked him up and took him to his mother’s house. He was still upset. She
stayed with him till 12.30 am or 1 am. 

 34. 
 Asked about Alex
seizures she said she had been his girlfriend since August of 2024. Since then,
she has seen him have three seizures including two in September. The other she
couldn’t remember the month but said it was the cooler months. She believes
that the emotional distress caused by Miss Thompson’s actions played a
significant role in triggering Alex’s seizure on Christmas Day. She says the
experience has left a lasting impact on her and Alex. 

 Miss Newall 

 35. 
 Miss Newall works
at the Pub. On Christmas Eve she was having dinner at the Pub with her father
and one of her father’s friends. In her statement she said Alex came up to her
and said “’ you alright darling?’ whilst giving me a hug. I found this quite
strange as he would never approach me like this sober” . She said in evidence that when he came up to
the table his eyes were quite glossy, which she took to be a sign of
intoxication. 

 36. 
 After dinner she
went into the public bar. Alex was there with his friend RS. She saw Alex
unsteadily approaching the bar with his friend RS. Miss Thompson told him that
he had had too much to drink and would not be served any more alcohol. He got
in a huff and continued to argue with Miss Thompson about having another beer.
Miss Thompson made it clear that he wouldn’t be served anything further, but he
could have a J20. 

 37. 
 She described
the argument as heated but a bit silly, back and forth and lasting about five
minutes. She couldn’t remember much of what was said but she didn’t recollect
any swearing. About 15 minutes later she went outside and heard Alex on the
phone to one of his parents saying he was going to “ take this to court” .

 38. 
 During cross -examination by Alex she said
that lots of people in the Pub were drunk that night. Asked why he was singled
out she said she didn’t know, it wasn’t her decision, she wasn’t working. 

 39. 
 In
cross-examination she was asked by Alex if she was aware of a rule that he was
only allowed to be served a certain number of drinks because of his epilepsy. She
responded that when Alex worked at the Pub the rule was two pints 
and that everyone knew that rule. The rule did not continue when Alex stopped
working there. Asked by the panel the reason for the rule, Miss Newall said
that it was due to his epilepsy. Asked what the rule was for other staff, Miss
Newall said that none of them were allowed to get intoxicated. Asked again by
Alex if there was a rule of two pints because of his epilepsy she said “ yes” .
She confirmed this during re -examination. 

 Mr Parry 

 40. 
 Mr Parry is an
assistant manager at the Pub. He worked in the lounge bar from noon until 9 pm
on Christmas Eve. 

 41. 
 He starts his
witness statement by saying that his first encounter with Alex was while he was
working. Alex’s father and grandparents came into the Pub. He asked Mr Cooper
how Alex was and was told “ he has been drinking in town and is running late
to join them.” When asked about this
statement in cross -examination he changed his evidence
to say that Mr Cooper said Alex was late meeting his grandparents because he
was out. Mr Cooper did not say where Alex was, and he didn’t say that Alex was
out drinking. 

 42. 
 His statement
continues: - 

 “ When [Alex] turned up, around
7.30 pm, he already had a pint of lager and was showing signs of intoxication.
He sat with his Dad , Grandparents and his girlfriend,
his Dad brought him another pint.” 

 43. 
 When asked about
this statement Mr Parry conceded that he saw Alex with a pint. He said that
Alex drank Moretti or Stella. Although the name of the server on the till
receipt was for another member of staff he vaguely
remembered it was him who served Mr Cooper. He assumed that when Mr Cooper
bought a round of drinks one of those was for Alex. He did not
therefore see Alex have two drinks and he conceded
that he was not sure if Alex had one drink or two. Asked by the panel about the
 intoxication , Mr Parry said that he noticed this after
his shift when he was talking to Alex outside. He described him as stumbling
around and slurring his words. He said he could see it in Alex’s eyes. 

 44. 
 In his statement
he said that Alex, his father, grandparents and girlfriend sat together until
about 9 pm which is when Mr Parry finished his shift. He writes “ I let
Lauren know Alex is intoxicated.” Asked by the panel about the conversation
he said that it took place at the public bar. He told Miss Thompson that he thought
Alex was quite intoxicated, she asked if she should cut him off and he said make
your own judgment. 

 45. 
 He deals with
the refusal to serve very shortly in his statement saying that Alex tried to
order a Stella pint from Miss Thompson which she refused. She made it clear
that he had had enough and could have a soft drink. Alex wasn’t happy with this
response and tried to argue that he should be served. Asked to elaborate in
cross-examination he said that the incident occurred around 9.15 to 9.20 pm. Alex
was trying to order a beer Moretti which Mr Parry assumed was for Alex. Mr
Parry was sitting about a metre and a half away
talking to friends. He heard Miss Thompson refuse Alex
service because of intoxication. The tone of the conversation was normal bar
tone, no raised voices, more stubborn voices. Asked if there was any screaming
he said no. He said he didn’t hear any swearing. He thought the conversation
lasted about 10 minutes. After Alex was refused a drink by Miss Thompson Alex
bought a Guiness for Mr Parry. Mr Parry wasn’t aware of Alex buying drinks for
other friends. Alex bought a J2O for himself. 

 46. 
 Asked about the two-pint
rule, Mr Parry said that this was introduced in early 2024 after an incident
involving staff drinking in the Pub after hours. He said the rule applied to all
staff on the days they worked. It did not apply on days off. Asked if the rule
was put in place because of Alex’s seizures Mr Parry
said that he knew Alex had seizures but did not know it was because of
epilepsy. He said that Alex had two seizures. The two-pint rule was put in
place for everyone, and they were informed at a staff meeting. He believed that
the rule was “ mostly to protect staff and customers”. 

 47. 
 At the end of
his statement Mr Parry describes seeing Alex on the phone to his parents. He
says Alex was acting very frustrated and threatening to get lawyers involved.
He says that just before Alex left, he saw that he managed to get someone to
buy him a pint which he drank fast. Asked about this he said that Alex was on
the wall outside drinking a glass with what looked like lager .

 Miss
Thompson 

 48. 
 Miss Thompson
has worked for the First Respondent for over eight years. She was manager of
the Pub between September 2023 and February 2025. She has had training on the
provisions of the staff handbook. She has had no training on the provisions of
the Discrimination Law that apply to the provision of goods and services or the
obligation to make reasonable adjustments for people with disabilities. 

 49. 
 She was working
in the restaurant until about 9 pm. Alex was in the lounge bar with his father,
grandparents and girlfriend. She briefly saw Alex to say hello to between 7.30
and 8 pm. At around 9 pm she started working in the lounge bar. As it was
Christmas Eve she wanted to let her staff finish early. Mr Parry told her that Alex
appeared to be drunk which she took on board. He then
sat at the bar with his friends. Miss Newall was also at the bar with her dad.
They were both within earshot when Alex approached the bar. In her statement
she says: - 

 “ This is when I observed the fitstate of [Alex], and like any other customer, before
serving, I deemed [Alex] to be intoxicated. With me being the only one on duty
at this time, I made the decision to cut [Alex] off, especially as he was
slurring his words. He then was not happy that this decision had been made and
tried to argue the fact he should be served. Like I would do in any case, I
told him again that no further alcohol would be served to him. This is when he
got mad and stormed out the pub shouting. Both Jamie
and Rose had been outside and seen [Alex] and overheard him on a phonecall . They made me aware he
was still unsteady on his feet and shouting. With any customers in this state,
I have always treatment them the same as this is the
correct procedure under the Licensing Law. I had prior knowledge of [Alex’s]
medical condition, as did the other staff, due to working with him. However,
this was not brought to attention or mentioned on that evening.” 

 50. 
 In oral evidence
she provided more detail. She said Alex was with a few friends including RS.
Alex stumbled and having previous knowledge of him, she had seen him drunk
before, she knew he’d had more than a few drinks. The signs were that he was
slurring his words “ mostly ” and a “ little” stumble. She also said
that from his eyes she could see that he had had more than a few drinks. 

 51. 
 He asked if he
could have a drink. Miss Thompson said no and did not give a reason to begin
with. He got mad and asked why and Miss Thompson said he was drunk. He tried to
argue back the case that he wasn’t. He was told he
could have a soft drink but not any alcohol. He asked if he could buy a drink
for his friends RS and Mr Parry. She said yes and that he could have a soft
drink. He ordered a J20. Miss Thompson kept the drinks for Mr Parry and RS and
did not hand them to Alex. 

 52. 
 When the
description of the conversation in the Response Form was put to her, she said
he wasn’t screaming but had a raised voice. He was getting annoyed 
and she asked him to calm down. Alex was loud enough to draw a bit of attention
although it was quite noisy in the bar. She claimed that Alex called her a
bitch. She said the whole conversation lasted for about 10 minutes. 

 53. 
 Asked about
whether she mentioned Alex’s epilepsy she said no. Asked why if he was so
intoxicated she did not ask him to leave she said she could have, but it was
Christmas Eve and she didn’t want him kicking off. 

 54. 
 Asked about
Alex’s epilepsy she said she only found out about it when he had a fit behind
the bar and “s aved your life twice”. Afterwards his parents asked her to reduce his
hours which she did with Alex’s agreement. She said that after the fit she only
gave him low alcohol beer and shandy. After a week or so she let him order what
he wanted. Asked if the discussions with Alex’s parents had
any input on her decision to refuse service she said absolutely
not , it did not cross her mind. Asked if she was consciously or unconsciously
influenced by the effect of alcohol on his health when she refused him service she said she didn’t agree with that. 

 55. 
 In relation to
whether there was a special two-pint rule that applied to Alex she denied this.
She said that there was two-pint rule for all staff that was put in place in
early 2024 after an incident where she was sick and the staff stayed drinking
in the Pub until the early hours. The rule ceased to apply to Alex when he
stopped being a member of staff and she only owed him a duty of care as a
customer. During the hearing Miss Thompson found a WhatsApp staff group chat message
from November 2024 saying, “ Staff will only be allowed 2 pints on the
premises on the days they’re working, after working hours.” Miss Thompson
said this was reaffirming an existing rule. 

 Documents 

 56. 
 We were shown a
small number of documents showing purchases on Christmas Eve. The Respondents
disclosed two till receipts. The first is timed at 8.05
pm and is for a pint and a half of Fullers London Pride and a quarter bottle of
wine for a total sum of £15.85. The second is timed at
10.19 pm and records the purchase of a Moretti, a Guiness and a J20 for the
total sum of £15.65. 

 57. 
 Alex disclosed a
bank statement that shows two purchases at the Blue Note for £6.75 and £10.15,
a purchase at the Pub for £15.65 and a purchase at Roberts Garage Bel Royal for
£40.70. 

 58. 
 Mr Cooper
disclosed a credit card statement that shows two purchases at the Pub, one for
£5.25 and one for £15.85. 

 Findings of Fact 

 59. 
 We have
considered carefully all the witness evidence, the documents and the phone
records. We have paid particular attention to the till receipts, the bank
records and the call logs. We find the sequences of
events was as follows: - 

 a. 
 Alex left work
at 2 pm and walked into town where he went to the Blue Note as evidenced by his
bank statement. 

 b. 
 Alex left town
and went to his father’s house on The Avenue arriving between 3 pm and 4 pm. 

 c. 
 Between 3 pm and
4 pm Miss Debois met Alex at his father’s house. 

 d. 
 Probably between
5 pm and 6 pm possibly a bit later Miss Desbois took
Alex to his mother’s house on Wellington Road. They went via Roberts Garage Bel
Royal where Alex paid for something. 

 e. 
 Alex stayed at
his mother’s house until about 9 pm. He caught the bus to St Martin and arrived
at the Pub about 9.30 pm. Shortly afterwards his father bought him one drink. We
consider that the repeated calls from Mr Cooper to Alex that continued until 8.34
pm show that Alex did not arrive at the Pub until much
later than several of the witnesses suggest. 

 f. 
 Miss Debois
joined Alex and his family for a short time arriving about 9.30 pm and leaving
about 10 pm. 

 g. 
 Mr Cooper and
Alex’s grandparents left the Pub around 10 pm. 

 h. 
 Just before
10:19 pm Alex approached Miss Thompson at the bar. She allowed him to buy two alcoholic
drinks for his friends including a Guiness for Mr
Parry. She refused to allow him to buy an alcoholic drink for himself and only
allowed him a non-alcoholic drink. 

 i. 
 There was a
heated debate between Alex and Miss Thompson that lasted between five and ten
minutes. 

 j. 
 Alex went
outside and called his father at 10.40 pm. He also called Miss Debois who
picked him up and took him to his mother’s house. 

 60. 
 On the question
of how much Alex had to drink prior to being refused service we find that he
had only two alcoholic drinks; a Prosecco with orange juice between 5 pm and 9
pm and a pint of lager that he started drinking after 9.30 pm. We make this
finding because we accept Alex’s evidence that while he bought drinks for
others at the Blue Note he himself did not have a
drink. Had he been drinking we think it likely that
Miss Desbois or Ms Cooper would have been aware of it. There is no evidence
that Alex had anything to drink while he was with Miss Desbois at Mr Cooper’s
house. We also accept Alex’s evidence corroborated by
the evidence of Ms Cooper that he had one glass of Prosecco
mixed with orange juice while at his mother’s house. We find that Alex had one
pint of lager between arriving at the Pub around 9.30 pm and being refused
service just before 10.19 pm. This is the drink that was bought for him by his
father. We reject the evidence of Mr Parry that Alex had two drinks in the Pub
because he accepted in cross examination that this was assumed and not
something he had seen. We also reject the evidence of Miss Desbois that Alex
had two drinks in the Pub as she was only there a
short time and she only saw him with one drink. 

 61. 
 As to how Alex
presented when he asked to be served alcohol by Miss Thompson at approximately
10.19 pm we find that he did not show obvious signs of
intoxication. We find that he was not glassy eyed, stumbling or slurring his
words. We find that Miss Thompson, Mr Parry and Miss Newall exaggerated their
evidence regarding how Alex presented. Mr
Parry accepted in cross-examination that statements he made in his witness
statement relating to Alex drinking in town and having had two drinks in the
Pub were assumptions and not something he knew. We consider that Miss Thompson
exaggerated to protect herself and we note that
descriptions of the interaction with Alex after the refusal set out in the Response
Form filed on her behalf were clear exaggerations e.g. that Alex was screaming
at her. We find Miss Newall’s evidence on his presentation to be unreliable and
intended to protect Miss Thompson. We prefer the evidence of Alex, Mr Cooper
and Ms Desbois that Alex did not show any obvious signs of intoxication .
This is consistent with our previous finding that he had only two alcoholic drinks
that day. 

 62. 
 We consider next
whether Miss Thompson, when refusing to serve Alex, referred to his epilepsy
and that she did so in front of people he did not know. On this allegation we
find that Alex has not shown on the balance of probability that the comment was
made. We accept the evidence of Miss Thompson that she did not refer to his
epilepsy. We prefer her evidence to that of Alex which
is not corroborated by any evidence from RS or AS. And we also rejected Alex’s
evidence that Miss Thompson told him to “ fuck-off ” which based on his
own evidence that he had never heard her swear before was out of character for
her. 

 63. 
 We also find
that Miss Thompson did not tell other staff not to serve Alex alcohol. By 10.19
pm she was the only person working behind the lounge bar and we find it
unlikely that she would have issued this instruction. 

 Decision on liability 

 64. 
 We are
satisfied that refusing service of an
alcoholic drink in a public house is less favourable treatment. 

 65. 
 The next question
we must answer is whether the refusal was because of Alex’s protected
characteristic of disability. We remind ourselves that it
is not necessary for his epilepsy to be the sole or main reason for the less
favourable treatment as long as it was a significant
influence. 

 66. 
 We find that a hypothetical
comparator who presented as Alex presented i.e. no obvious signs of
intoxication but without epilepsy would not have been refused an alcoholic
drink by Miss Thompson. We therefore find that the reason for the less
favourable treatment was Alex’s epilepsy. We are supported in our conclusion by
the statement in the Response Form that Miss Thompson was always mindful of
Alex’s condition and her evidence that she did not serve him alcohol for a
period after a seizure. We accept her evidence that
the two-pint rule applied to all staff after January 2024. We also accept the
evidence of Miss Newall that before that there was a two-pint rule that applied
to Alex which was linked to his epilepsy. We consider that concern for Alex’s
condition continued after he stopped working at the Pub and that this
influenced Miss Thompson’s decision to refuse him service. In our view she was
ultra cautious with him because she believed that his condition was exacerbated
by alcohol, a point that is made in the Response Form. Having been told by Mr
Parry that Alex had had too much to drink she refused him service despite him
showing no obvious signs of intoxication . 

 67. 
 We find that in
refusing Alex service of an alcohol drink she discriminated against him on the
grounds of his protected characteristic of disability contrary to Article 6(1).

 68. 
 Given our finding above on the claim of direct discrimination under
Article 6(1) we do not consider it necessary to consider whether the refusal
also constituted harassment under Article 28. 

 69. 
 We have already
found that Miss Thompson did not refer to epilepsy when refusing to serve Alex
an alcoholic drink. Any claim of discrimination based on a reference to his
epilepsy in front of people therefore fails. 

 70. 
 We have also
found that Miss Thompson did not tell other staff not to serve Alex. Any claim
of discrimination arising from this allegation fails. 

 Decision
on quantum 

 71. 
 Having
considered the guidance in Flanagan we consider that this case falls
within the middle band. We have considered that the refusal was public in front
of his friends, Alex 
was very upset by the refusal and he no longer feels
comfortable visiting the Pub. We are satisfied that the refusal of service
contributed to the seizure on Christmas Day. We are not satisfied that the
seizure on Christmas Day led to a significantly longer driving exclusion
because Miss Desbois said that there had been seizures in September 2024 and at
least one in the cooler months. Taking all these points into account, we award
Alex compensation of £2,500. 

 Apportionment 

 72. 
 We can apportion
compensation between the Respondents as we consider just and equitable. We
order that 100% of the compensation should be paid by the First Respondent. We
are surprised that in eight years of working for the First Respondent Miss
Thompson has received no discrimination training. We consider that had
appropriate training been put in place she would have understood that her actions
arising from her concerns about Alex’s wellbeing, no matter how well
intentioned, were discriminatory. The right of any manager to refuse service
must be exercised in a manner that is compliant with the provisions of the
Discrimination Law. 

 Conclusion 

 73. 
 The First
Respondent is ordered to pay Alex compensation in the
sum of £2,500 within 14 days of the date of this judgment. 

 Advocate
Dr Elena Moran, Chairman Date : 
 23
May 2025 

 Page Last Updated: 12 Sep 2025
